title: Neonatal care for multiple lambs
subtitle: Managing twins and triplets through the first 24 hours, when competition for colostrum, warmth, and teat access determines which lambs survive.
excerpt: Twins and triplets are the productive heart of a profitable sheep enterprise, but they require more management in the first day of life than singles. Each lamb must receive colostrum, establish a teat, and maintain its temperature. Triplets especially need the shepherd's intervention to ensure the third lamb is not squeezed out by its larger siblings.
sourceNotes: AHDB Beef and Lamb: Lambing Management guide. Moredun Research Institute lambing factsheets. DEFRA Code of Welfare for Sheep (2023).

glossaryTerms.definition: The first milk a ewe produces after lambing, rich in maternal antibodies and energy. Lambs must receive colostrum within the first two hours of life for passive immunity transfer; the intestinal permeability that allows antibody absorption closes by six hours. Without adequate colostrum, lambs have no disease protection.

A ewe that fails to bond with, or actively rejects, one of her lambs. Common in triplet litters where the ewe may bond firmly with the first two lambs born and treat the third as a stranger. Also seen when labour was long, the lamb is weak, or the ewe is a first-time mother.

A soft rubber or plastic tube passed through the lamb's mouth and oesophagus into the rumen to deliver colostrum or electrolytes directly to the stomach when a lamb cannot suck. An essential lambing kit item for hypothermic or weak lambs.

content.text: When a ewe lambs with triplets, the first lamb born has a head start of 20 to 40 minutes on the third. By the time the third lamb is dry and mobile, its siblings may already have drunk twice. The shepherd's job in the first hour is to equalise that disadvantage by ensuring the third lamb gets
colostrum
before the ewe's production peaks and begins to decline, and before hypothermia sets in.
Immediately after birth
Check each lamb is breathing. Clear mucus from mouth and nostrils with your finger or a piece of clean straw.
Spray all navels within the first 10 minutes using
iodine navel spray
. Joint ill enters through the navel; treating early is the prevention.
Weigh all three lambs if your kit includes scales. A lamb under 3 kg needs extra attention on colostrum intake and warmth.
Ensuring all three lambs get colostrum

Steady the ewe against the pen wall and guide each lamb to a teat in turn. Hold the lamb on the teat for 30 seconds, enough for it to make the suckling connection and continue on its own. The smallest lamb should be assisted first, or given its turn while the others are held back briefly.
If a lamb is weak or the ewe is uncooperative, use a
stomach tube

to deliver 50 ml per kg bodyweight of colostrum directly. A 4 kg lamb needs 200 ml in the first two hours. Cow colostrum or commercial frozen sheep colostrum are acceptable if the ewe's own production is insufficient.
Managing mismothering
Ewes that deliver triplets often bond solidly with the first two and treat the third with suspicion or hostility.
Mismothering

of a triplet shows as the ewe pushing the third lamb away when it approaches a teat, or simply moving away from it consistently. Keeping the family in a small individual pen (1.5 m x 1.5 m) reduces the ewe's ability to escape the lamb and gives the relationship time to establish.

If the ewe consistently refuses the third lamb after 12 hours, the practical decision is to foster it onto another ewe or raise it as a bottle lamb. Prolonged fighting for a bond that is not forming is stressful for both the ewe and the lamb. A weak triplet that has not fed adequately in the first 6 hours has a rapidly declining prognosis; intervention beats waiting.
The decision on triplets: twin on, bottle one

Most experienced shepherds leave triplets on the ewe for 24 hours, then move the smallest to a foster ewe or bottle. A ewe with good milk can raise triplets with supplementary creep feed from week two, but triplet lambs wean lighter than twins and need close monitoring.
